stories.title: Gerrit Cole Returns Tonight — 569 Days Ends at Yankee Stadium (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
569 days since his last MLB pitch.

Gerrit Cole starts TONIGHT against the Rays at Yankee Stadium.

Tommy John surgery. Triple-A rehab. 97 mph. 99.6 mph peak.

The ace is back. ⚾ #Yankees

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
The Rays lead the AL East by 4.5 games.

Tonight, the Yankees get their ace back.

Gerrit Cole vs. the team blocking them for first place.

First pitch tonight at Yankee Stadium. ⚾ #Yankees #Rays

facebook.long_form:
⚾ 569 DAYS. TONIGHT IT ENDS. ⚾

Gerrit Cole's last pitch in the major leagues was in Game 5 of the 2024 World Series.

Then came the news. Tommy John surgery, March 2025. A 14-month rebuild. Pitch counts that started at 15 throws and worked back toward what an MLB starter looks like.

Tonight, it's done. Cole starts for the New York Yankees against the Tampa Bay Rays at Yankee Stadium — his first major league appearance in 569 days.

The arm came back. In his most recent Triple-A start, Cole averaged 97 miles per hour on his fastball and peaked at 99.6. He threw 86 pitches across 5 1/3 innings. The elbow is healed and the velocity is back. That's not guaranteed after Tommy John surgery. Cole earned it.

The stakes make this bigger. Tampa Bay leads the AL East at 31-15 — 4.5 games ahead of New York. The Yankees at 30-20 have been a winning team without their ace. Now imagine what they become with him.

This is the start Yankees fans have been waiting for since October 2024.

[Will Cole be as dominant as he was before the surgery? Tell us what you expect tonight!] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: 569 days since his last MLB pitch. Gerrit Cole starts TONIGHT at Yankee Stadium vs. the AL East-leading Rays. The ace is back. ⚾ #Yankees

stories.title: Murakami Has Homered in 5 Straight Games — 3 Away from an MLB Record (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Munetaka Murakami has homered in 5 straight games.

That ties the White Sox franchise record.

He is 3 games away from tying the MLB all-time record (8 straight — Griffey, Mattingly, Long).

The White Sox rookie is one of the hottest hitters in baseball right now. ⚾ #WhiteSox

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
The all-time MLB record: 8 consecutive games with a home run.

Set by Ken Griffey Jr. (1993), Don Mattingly (1987), and Dale Long (1956).

Munetaka Murakami needs 3 more.

No pressure. Just the stuff of baseball legend. ⚾ #WhiteSox

facebook.long_form:
⚾ THREE MORE. THREE. ⚾

Munetaka Murakami has just done something no White Sox player has done since 2012: homer in five consecutive games. Five in a row. He just tied the Chicago White Sox franchise record.

A.J. Pierzynski. Paul Konerko. Carlos Lee. Frank Thomas (twice). Ron Kittle. Greg Luzinski. Those are the names who held it before him. Now Murakami is in that company.

But here's the thing — he's not stopping there. The all-time MLB record is 8 consecutive games with a home run, held by three of the most recognizable names in baseball history: Ken Griffey Jr. (1993), Don Mattingly (1987), and Dale Long (1956).

Murakami needs three more.

The White Sox were not supposed to be in the national baseball conversation this year. Yet here they are, with a Japanese-born rookie who hit more home runs in his first 21 MLB games than any player from Japan who came before him — including Shohei Ohtani.

Three games. One historic record. The least-expected storyline of the 2026 MLB season.

[Are you watching White Sox games to see if Murakami can get to 8?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Munetaka Murakami has homered in 5 straight games — tied for the White Sox franchise record. He needs just 3 more to tie the MLB all-time mark (8 straight), set by Griffey, Mattingly, and Dale Long. ⚾ #WhiteSox

stories.title: Nick Kurtz's Walk Streak Ends at 20 — Just Short of Cullenbine's Record (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Nick Kurtz's walk streak is over.

20 consecutive games. Tied with Barry Bonds for 2nd-longest all-time.

Two games short of Roy Cullenbine's record, set in 1947.

The most disciplined plate approach of the 2026 season came up just short of history. ⚾ #Athletics

facebook.long_form:
⚾ IT'S OVER. AND IT WAS HISTORIC. ⚾

Nick Kurtz walked in 20 consecutive games.

Then Oakland lost to the Cleveland Guardians 14-6. And Kurtz didn't draw a walk.

The streak is over at 20 consecutive games — tied with Barry Bonds (2002-03) for the second-longest in MLB history, two games short of Roy Cullenbine's record of 22, set in Detroit in 1947.

Two games. That's the gap. If Kurtz had drawn one walk in each of those two games, his name would be etched next to Cullenbine's in the record books.

But here's what shouldn't get lost: what Kurtz did was remarkable. He led the major leagues in walks. Pitchers were throwing him strikes — his strike rate was 55.3% — and he was still walking. That kind of plate discipline doesn't come from patience alone. It comes from a hitter who understands what he's doing at the plate at a level that most major leaguers never achieve.

Roy Cullenbine's record has stood for 79 years. It almost fell this week. Nobody saw it coming.

[Were you following Kurtz's walk streak? Were you rooting for him to break the record?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Nick Kurtz's 20-game consecutive walk streak is over — ending two games short of Roy Cullenbine's all-time record (1947). Tied with Barry Bonds for 2nd-longest ever. A remarkable run by Oakland's first baseman. ⚾ #Athletics

stories.title: Schwarber's 20-HR May — The 60-HR Club Math Is Now Real (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Kyle Schwarber hit his 20th home run on May 15.

That's the earliest any player in MLB history has done it.

He's on pace for 66+ HRs.

The 60-HR club: Bonds (73), McGwire (70), Sosa (66). Three members. One conversation. ⚾ #Phillies

facebook.long_form:
⚾ ARE WE REALLY DOING THIS? ⚾

Kyle Schwarber hit his 20th home run on May 15. Not a big deal on its own — except that it's the earliest any player in the history of Major League Baseball has reached 20 home runs.

Earlier than Barry Bonds in his 73-home run season. Earlier than Mark McGwire. Earlier than Sammy Sosa. Earlier than all of them.

Through his first 49 games of 2026, Schwarber is on pace for 66 home runs.

Let's put that in context. The 60-HR club has three members: Bonds (73), McGwire (70), and Sosa (66). No player has joined the club since 2001 — 25 years ago. The current generation of fans has essentially never watched a player seriously threaten 60 home runs in a clean season.

Schwarber is doing it.

The Phillies outfielder isn't just a power hitter running hot. He set a milestone on the calendar that no one in the history of baseball has cleared before. The numbers are not just impressive. They are unprecedented in when they arrived.

Is the pace sustainable? Maybe not. But the conversation is legitimate. The math is real. And Schwarber is the reason you should be watching the Phillies every night.

[Will Schwarber reach 60? What's your over/under on his final HR total?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Kyle Schwarber hit his 20th HR on May 15 — the earliest any MLB player ever has. He's on pace for 66+. The 60-HR club has 3 members, all from the late 1990s-2001. Schwarber is the next conversation. ⚾ #Phillies

stories.title: Ramírez Passes Averill, Closes in on 300-300 Club (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
José Ramírez just passed Earl Averill for No. 2 in Cleveland franchise history for home runs at home (138).

He also has 300 career stolen bases.

He is 9 home runs from 300 career HRs.

The 300-300 club: Mays, Bonds, A-Rod, Beltran, Dawson. Ramírez is next. ⚾ #Guardians

facebook.long_form:
⚾ PAYING ATTENTION TO JOSÉ RAMÍREZ YET? ⚾

Quietly, patiently, one game at a time, José Ramírez keeps adding to one of the most extraordinary statistical resumes in the history of Cleveland baseball.

This week: Ramírez passed Earl Averill for sole possession of second place on Cleveland's all-time home run list at home, with 138 career home runs at Progressive Field. Jim Thome, the all-time leader, has 184. Ramírez is in the conversation.

But the bigger number is 300. Ramírez is 9 home runs away from 300 career home runs. He already has 300 career stolen bases — reaching that milestone on May 2.

When he hits No. 300, he will join one of the most exclusive clubs in the history of the sport. The 300-300 club — players with both 300 career home runs and 300 career stolen bases — has 8 members: Willie Mays, Barry Bonds, Alex Rodriguez, Carlos Beltran, Andre Dawson, Deion Sanders, Steve Finley, and Bobby Bonds.

Nine home runs from belonging to that group.

Nine.

[Does Ramírez get there this season? Are you watching his countdown?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: José Ramírez is 9 HRs away from the 300-300 club (300 HR + 300 SB). He already has 300 stolen bases. He just passed Earl Averill for No. 2 on Cleveland's all-time home HR list. Nine home runs from history. ⚾ #Guardians

stories.title: This Week's Most Unusual MLB Stats (Tier 3)

x_posts.label: Post 6
x_posts.text:
Three MLB stats worth knowing this week:

• Matt Olson: 833 consecutive games (9th in MLB history)
• Mets scored 10 runs in a single extra inning on May 18 (4th ever)
• Schwarber leads Judge by 4 HRs — largest gap at this point in the modern era

⚾ Baseball is wild.

facebook.long_form:
⚾ THREE STATS FROM THIS WEEK THAT DESERVE YOUR ATTENTION ⚾

Sometimes baseball's best stories are buried in the box scores and the stat trackers. Here are three from the week ending May 22 that are worth knowing:

**1. Matt Olson has played 833 consecutive games.**

That's the 9th-longest consecutive games streak in MLB history. He hasn't missed a game since May 2, 2021 — over five years ago. Nobody talks about it. He just shows up.

**2. The Mets scored 10 runs in the 12th inning on May 18.**

This is one of the rarest things in baseball history — scoring 10 or more runs in a single extra inning has only happened seven times since the 1880s. The Mets are one of those seven. The 1983 Rangers still hold the record with 12 runs in the 15th inning.

**3. Schwarber leads Aaron Judge by 4 home runs — the largest gap between first and second place at this point in a season in the modern era.**

Judge has 16 home runs. He would be leading most years. In 2026, it's not even close.

Baseball is a sport that rewards paying attention to the numbers.

[Which of these three is the most incredible to you?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Matt Olson: 833 consecutive games. Mets: 10 runs in one extra inning. Schwarber leads Judge by 4 HRs. Three baseball facts from this week that most fans don't know. ⚾ This is why baseball is the best sport.
